summary:
Nevali Cori was a Pre-Pottery Neolithic B (PPNB) settlement located in southeastern Anatolia, on the Euphrates River in modern Turkey, occupied from approximately 8400–7600 BCE. The site was excavated in the 1980s before being submerged by the Ataturk Dam reservoir. It is notable for its sophisticated cult building, which contained T-shaped limestone pillars similar to those at Gobekli Tepe, carved with abstract reliefs and anthropomorphic features including carved arms and hands on their surfaces.

Nevali Cori is considered part of a cluster of sites in the upper Euphrates region that developed early monumental ritual architecture during the Pre-Pottery Neolithic. The settlement also yielded early evidence for textile production and some of the earliest known stone sculpture in the round, including a human head carved from limestone. Along with Gobekli Tepe and Cayonu, it forms part of the archaeological picture of an extraordinarily creative period in early Anatolian prehistory.
